one_line_takeaway: Sparse autoencoders on reward-model activations expose a small set of safety features that enable both a stealthy, chat-preserving preference-data poisoning attack (1B safety 92.3->71.8 at 5% label flips with chat intact) and a mirror-image denoising defense.
key_finding: SAFER delivers precise, targeted poisoning: on the 1B reward model at a 5% flip rate it drops RewardBench safety from 92.30 to 71.76 while keeping chat at 91.34 (essentially unchanged from the 89.39 baseline), whereas the Reward-based baseline at 5% reaches safety 77.70 but collapses chat to 57.54, and Random barely moves either metric (safety 92.84, chat 84.08). The specificity - large safety damage with negligible chat collateral - is the headline result. In defensive denoising, SAFER raises safety above baseline: best 1B safety 94.20 at 4% removal and best 3B safety 96.46 at 4% (vs 3B baseline 94.86), outperforming both baselines. Feature-level validation: GPT-4o's safety ratings match human ratings on >80% of 500 sampled features (|delta|=0) with no discrepancy >2, and SAFER-selected high-|si| features concentrate near the max relevance score of 5 versus a broad spread for randomly sampled features.
core_contribution: SAFER (Sparse Autoencoder For Enhanced Reward model) applies a TopK sparse autoencoder to the hidden activations of an RLHF reward model to extract sparse, human-interpretable, monosemantic features, then ranks those features by a contrastive safety score (the normalized activation difference between chosen and rejected responses). GPT-4o labels the top-ranked features for safety relevance, yielding a small set of safety-related feature indices. These indices are used to score every preference pair (scoresafe, Eq. 9), which then drives two feature-level data interventions: targeted label-flipping poisoning of the safest pairs, and denoising by removing the least-safe pairs. The contribution is the SAE-based interpretability lens on reward models PLUS the feature-guided data-manipulation pipeline built on it; the SAE machinery (TopK SAE from Gao et al. 2024) and the reward-based poisoning baseline (Pathmanathan et al. 2024) are prior work, not the paper's own novelty.
threat_model: Preference-data poisoning of the RLHF reward-model training pipeline. An attacker with the ability to alter a small fraction (0.5%-5%) of preference-pair labels uses SAFER's feature-level scoresafe to select the pairs that most strongly reinforce safety and flips their chosen/rejected labels before reward-model fine-tuning. The goal is targeted, stealthy degradation: sharply reduce the reward model's safety alignment while leaving general chat performance essentially untouched, so the sabotage is hard to detect via standard capability evals. The same machinery is presented in a defensive mode (denoising) to remove low-quality/misaligned safety pairs and improve reward-model reliability. The paper's Impact Statement explicitly acknowledges the poisoning method could be a blueprint for real attacks on alignment.
limitations: Scope is narrow: only LLaMA-3.2-1B/3B reward models and two safety datasets (PKU-SafeRLHF, WildGuardMix), evaluated solely on RewardBench safety/chat subsets - no test of downstream RLHF-aligned policy behavior, only the reward model itself. The safety-relevance labeling depends on GPT-4o as an oracle (validated only against human agreement on 500 features), so a systematic GPT-4o bias would propagate. The pipeline hinges on the contrastive final-token heuristic and a single chosen layer (12) / sparsity setting; generalization beyond safety (helpfulness, reasoning) is stated as future work, not demonstrated. Denoising gains are non-monotonic and modest (a few points), and the authors note aggressive removal discards informative boundary cases. The paper offers no concrete defense against SAFER-style targeted poisoning beyond calling for future robustification. The full limitations are deferred to Appendix G (not in the reviewed pages).

datasets_used: WildGuardMix (safety/moderation preference dataset); combined ~102k preference pairs for RM training
OpenWebText2 (100M tokens, general corpus for stage-1 SAE pretraining)
quantitative_results: Baseline reward models: Llama-3.2-1B-RM safety 92.30 / chat 89.39; Llama-3.2-3B-RM safety 94.86 / chat 89.11 (RewardBench)
Poisoning 1B @5% flip: SAFER safety 71.76 / chat 91.34; Reward-based safety 77.70 / chat 57.54; Random safety 92.84 / chat 84.08
Poisoning 3B @5% flip: SAFER safety 75.14 / chat 90.50; Reward-based safety 81.08 / chat 54.47; Random safety 93.92 / chat 84.64
Denoising 3B: SAFER best safety 96.46 @4%; peak chat 92.07 @8%; baseline safety 94.86
SAE config: TopK, k=64, dictionary M=16,384 (8x expansion), residual stream at 3/4 depth (layer 12); 32 safety-related features retained
Feature validation: >80% of 500 sampled features got identical GPT-4o vs human scores (|delta|=0), ~15% with |delta|=1 or 2, none >2
Case study: Feature #1472 ranked 1st of 16,384 with contrastive score s=0.59, GPT-4o safety relevance 5 (harmful/discriminatory content)
Ablation (1B): pretrained-only SAE at 5% poisoning gives safety 73.78 vs SAFER 71.76 (weaker attack); sentence-level training outperforms token-level on denoising
baselines_compared: Random (uniformly selects a fixed proportion of pairs to poison/denoise)
Reward-based (Pathmanathan et al. 2024; selects by reward-margin difference between chosen and rejected)
Ablation baselines: Pretrained SAE (no safety fine-tuning), token-level vs sentence-level SAE training, layer-position variants (layers 4/8 vs 12), GPT-4o-mini / LLM-based selection
